summaries.headline: California Educators Push Integrated Math-Coding Approach
summaries.summary: California's new K-12 mathematics framework emphasizes equity and real-world relevance as the state grapples with just over one-third of students meeting math standards in 2025. UC Davis researchers advocate for teaching mathematics through integrated coding and robotics programs, drawing on more than two decades of development work with thousands of teachers to make abstract concepts tangible for diverse learners. The approach reflects growing recognition that computational thinking belongs alongside traditional literacy skills, particularly as rigid subject boundaries become less relevant in an AI-driven economy.

summaries.headline: Congress Rejects Trump Cuts, Maintains K-12 Funding
summaries.summary: Congress approved a fiscal 2026 budget that maintains level funding for virtually every existing K-12 program, rejecting the Trump administration's proposals to slash billions from federal education investments. The House voted 217-214 to pass the package after ending a partial government shutdown, with President Trump signing the bill into law shortly afterward. The narrow vote and year-long uncertainty over education funding signals the political challenges ahead for any administration seeking major changes to federal K-12 investments.

summaries.headline: Stanford AI Research Hub Hits 1,000 Education Studies
summaries.summary: Stanford University's AI Hub for Education Research Repository crossed 1,000 studies in January, adding 204 papers in a single update that represented nearly 25% growth in two months. The new research examined gender bias in automated essay feedback, student trust patterns with AI chatbots, and how young children perceive artificial intelligence tools. The repository's rapid expansion reflects the field's shift from pilot programs to broader AI implementation as schools seek evidence-based guidance for classroom integration decisions.

summaries.headline: New Jersey Confirms Literacy-Focused Education Commissioner
summaries.summary: The New Jersey Board of Education confirmed Lily Laux as acting commissioner of education, positioning her to lead the state's education department pending Senate approval. Laux brings experience from Texas, where she implemented science of reading training for more than 150,000 K-3 teachers and led school finance reform as deputy commissioner of school programs. Her appointment signals a state-level focus on literacy outcomes and tutoring initiatives as New Jersey looks to build on existing academic strengths.

summaries.headline: Women Leaders Face Glass Cliff in Education Roles
summaries.summary: Women in education leadership consistently receive assignments that place them in the most challenging schools and districts with the highest stakes and least margin for error, according to new survey data from Women Leading Ed. Fewer than 20 percent of responding principals had served in high school roles and just over one in 20 had held finance or operations positions, suggesting women are being steered away from pathway roles that typically lead to senior leadership. The organization argues that without systemic changes to recruitment, training, and advancement practices—including a shift from mentoring to active sponsorship—the gender gap in education leadership will persist.

summaries.headline: Digital Promise Launches $26 Million AI Infrastructure Program
summaries.summary: Digital Promise is leading a four-year, $26 million initiative to build open AI datasets and models specifically for K-12 education, with Penn GSE's Catalyst program as a core partner alongside Learning Data Insights and Georgetown's Massive Data Institute. The program addresses a growing gap between how students learn and how current generative AI tools are designed, requiring all funded outputs to be openly shared rather than proprietary. The initiative opens for public input later this month, signaling a shift toward collaborative governance as AI adoption in schools continues to outpace shared standards and evidence.

summaries.headline: Indiana Advances Cell Phone Restrictions, Other Bills
summaries.summary: Indiana lawmakers are advancing key education bills through the 2026 legislative session after withdrawing more controversial measures like requiring Ten Commandments displays and social media bans for minors. SB 78 would tighten the state's existing cell phone ban by requiring districts to either prohibit personal devices entirely or mandate storage during the full instructional day, while other advancing bills address math instruction, restroom access, and abstinence education. The streamlined legislative agenda reflects both political pragmatism and focused attention on classroom management issues that have broad support among educators and parents.

summaries.headline: NYC Pre-K Teachers Rally Against Pay Gap Before Expansion
summaries.summary: New York City pre-K teachers working in community-based programs plan to rally at City Hall next week, demanding pay equity before Mayor Zohran Mamdani expands early childhood services to 2-year-olds. A city comptroller audit found that 90% of community-based lead teachers with master's degrees still earn less than their public school counterparts, with some certified teachers in the Bronx and Brooklyn starting at less than $36,000 annually — nearly $30,000 below public school rates. The protest highlights how wage disparities in the city's dual early childhood system continue undermining teacher retention and program quality despite years of promises to address the inequities.

summaries.headline: Student Testing Reveals EdTech Design Blind Spots
summaries.summary: Researchers are increasingly involving students directly in edtech product testing, uncovering a consistent disconnect between what impresses adults in vendor demonstrations and what actually works for young users. Student feedback reveals that tools designed primarily for administrative convenience or adult approval often fail to engage learners or support their actual learning needs. These findings suggest district leaders may need to build student voice into their technology evaluation processes to avoid investing in products that look promising to adults but fall short in the classroom.

summaries.headline: Multilingual Students Need Comprehension Beyond Phonics Focus
summaries.summary: Multilingual learners often master decoding skills but struggle with reading comprehension, revealing gaps in current literacy instruction that prioritizes phonics over meaning-making. These students can sound out words fluently yet lack the background knowledge, vocabulary, and cultural context needed to understand what they read, creating a hidden literacy challenge that standardized assessments may not capture. The disconnect suggests schools need instructional approaches that build comprehension alongside decoding skills, particularly for the growing population of students learning English while developing literacy.

summaries.headline: Ohio School Funding Rises Despite District Crisis Claims
summaries.summary: Ohio districts and media outlets are painting a dire picture of school finances, with superintendents claiming they're losing money and headlines describing "devastating" cuts. State data tells a different story: inflation-adjusted per-pupil spending has climbed 17.3 percent from roughly $14,500 to $17,000 between 2013 and 2025, with traditional districts seeing slightly higher increases at 18.5 percent than charter schools and career-tech centers. The gap between public messaging and spending reality suggests districts may be leveraging crisis narratives during budget negotiations rather than confronting underlying operational efficiency questions.

summaries.headline: AI Assistance Weakens Student Skill Development in Programming
summaries.summary: Anthropic researchers found that developers learning a new Python library with AI support scored 17 percent lower on assessments measuring conceptual understanding, code reading, and debugging compared to those working without AI assistance. The AI-assisted group showed no significant improvement in task completion time, while those who relied heavily on AI for code generation and debugging demonstrated the weakest learning outcomes. The findings suggest that productivity tools designed to speed up work may undermine the deeper learning processes students need to develop independent problem-solving capabilities.

summaries.headline: Colorado's First Public Christian School Closes Building Immediately
summaries.summary: Riverstone Academy, which backers call Colorado's first public Christian school, agreed to close its building in Pueblo County after county officials threatened to shut off utilities due to health and safety violations, including inadequate fire safety systems. The closure affects about 30 kindergarten through fifth grade students and comes as the controversial school faces a state audit over its eligibility for public funding. The abrupt shutdown represents a significant setback for a school that was created quietly last summer and appears designed to test whether public money can fund religious education in the courts.

summaries.headline: Jordan Deploys AI Reading Tutors Nationwide
summaries.summary: Jordan's Ministry of Education launched a national literacy initiative using AI-supported reading technology across primary schools, targeting grades four and seven students with personalized tutoring sessions three to five times per week. The pilot program uses Amira Learning's platform to provide immediate feedback during 10-15 minute reading sessions while generating assessment data for teachers, with plans to reach up to 800,000 students if scaled nationally. The systematic deployment of AI tutoring at national scale offers a model for other countries weighing how to integrate artificial intelligence into core literacy instruction rather than treating it as supplemental technology.

summaries.headline: Kansas Districts Stockpile Cash While Test Scores Plummet
summaries.summary: Kansas school districts spent a record $18,858 per student in 2024-25 while accumulating $3.1 billion in cash reserves, yet only 17% of graduates tested college-ready in all four core subjects compared to 32% a decade ago. Districts cut 347 classroom teachers but added over 1,150 non-teaching staff as enrollment dropped nearly 3,000 students, with instruction receiving just 52% of total spending despite state guidance calling it the foundation of education. The widening gap between record spending and declining outcomes raises questions about how districts allocate resources when student achievement matters most.

summaries.headline: Districts Add Staff While Enrollment Drops Nationwide
summaries.summary: Public schools added 118,000 employees last year while serving 135,000 fewer students, continuing a post-pandemic trend that has districts hiring aggressively amid declining enrollment. Since 2018-19, student enrollment has dropped 1.4 million students while employment has risen by 479,000 positions, with most of the growth—389,000 jobs—coming from non-teaching roles including administrators and support staff rather than classroom teachers. The persistent mismatch between staffing growth and enrollment decline suggests districts are either banking on enrollment recovery or fundamentally restructuring their operations around smaller class sizes and expanded support services.

summaries.headline: High School Internship Program Expands Corporate Placements
summaries.summary: Genesys Works connects high school students to paid corporate internships across eight cities, moving beyond typical retail and food service jobs to place teens in professional environments like tech helpdesks and office settings. The nonprofit, which launched in Houston in 2002, provides summer training to build students' communication and technical skills before matching them with companies seeking entry-level support. Students like Minneapolis senior Najaax Sheikh Ali report significant confidence gains while gaining exposure to career paths they previously considered out of reach.

summaries.headline: Standardized Privacy Agreements Level Student Data Protection
summaries.summary: Large districts with legal teams have long negotiated robust vendor data privacy terms while smaller districts—often with one or two staff handling IT, procurement, and security—operated under the same regulatory requirements without comparable resources or leverage. The Student Data Privacy Consortium's standardized agreements now support 34 million students across 34 statewide alliances, allowing districts to adopt pre-negotiated terms rather than recreate contracts for similar data use cases. This shift from local contract interpretation to shared infrastructure reduces the operational burden on under-resourced districts and creates more consistent privacy protections regardless of district size or staffing capacity.

summaries.headline: Idaho Governor Shields Education From State Budget Cuts
summaries.summary: Idaho Gov. Brad Little is defending his budget proposal that protects education funding from reductions, even as state lawmakers have instructed the Department of Education to prepare for potential cuts amid a projected shortfall after years of surplus. The governor's "Enduring Idaho" budget shields schools while directing other state agencies to prepare for additional cuts. The budget standoff highlights the growing tension between maintaining education investments and managing tighter state finances as Idaho transitions from years of historic surpluses.

summaries.headline: Idaho Superintendent Refuses Lawmakers' School Budget Cut Request
summaries.summary: State Superintendent Debbie Critchfield declined to propose additional budget cuts for Idaho's $3 billion public schools budget after legislative leaders asked her to identify 1% to 2% reductions for the current and next fiscal years. Critchfield had already revised her budget downward, removing a $50 million special education request and making other cuts to align with enrollment data, while warning that further reductions could mean larger class sizes and legal risks for unmet special education needs. Her refusal sets up a potential standoff with lawmakers grappling with budget deficits, even as Governor Brad Little has exempted schools from the 3% cuts imposed on other state agencies.

synthesis.topic_title: Literacy Expands Beyond Reading: States Embrace Comprehensive Approaches
synthesis.whats_happening: School districts are broadening literacy instruction beyond traditional reading to include financial, health, and news literacy as core curriculum requirements. Community partnerships are scaling up to support these expanded literacy demands, with volunteer programs mobilizing hundreds of participants to supplement classroom instruction.
synthesis.whats_driving: Digital transformation has created new literacy demands that traditional reading instruction doesn't address, forcing districts to adapt their approaches. Post-pandemic learning gaps have intensified the need for community-supported literacy interventions as schools recognize they cannot tackle literacy challenges alone.

- Community volunteer programs are becoming essential literacy infrastructure, with organized initiatives mobilizing over 1,000 volunteers in single events

- Professional development priorities are evolving to address diverse literacy demands in digital contexts, requiring new educator training approaches

# New state financial literacy standards implementing smoothly at local level, say Grand Forks school leaders

## As national personal debt continues to rise, North Dakota implemented statewide financial literacy content standards for students K-12

![2180304+Money.jpg](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

istock photos

[![Sophia Herman](<Base64-Image-Removed>)](https://www.grandforksherald.com/sophia-herman)

By [Sophia Herman](https://www.grandforksherald.com/sophia-herman)

January 31, 2026 at 8:20 AM

[Comments](https://www.grandforksherald.com/news/local/new-state-financial-literacy-standards-implementing-smoothly-at-local-level-say-grand-forks-school-leaders#conversations)

Share


News Reporting


GRAND FORKS — After the North Dakota Department of Public Instruction released statewide financial literacy content standards for K-12 students, Grand Forks school leaders say the implementation is going well.

In the third quarter of 2025, Americans' non-housing debt rose to

[$5.09 trillion, a steady increase from $2.85 trillion in 2013](https://www.newyorkfed.org/microeconomics/hhdc)

, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of New York. As national personal debt continues to rise, North Dakota implemented the first standalone set of requirements developed by the state to improve students’ financial literacy. The implementation is new as of the current school year.

Davonne Eldredge, NDDPI assistant director of academic support, said making kids aware of their spending habits and the impact of those habits can help them make financially responsible decisions in adulthood.

“If you look at when we get to be adults, you will see that adults face decisions regarding how they are going to spend their money. They are influenced by things like social media. They may be influenced by advertisements that they see on television,” she said. “If we start helping our students see that those factors can play a role in their lives when they're young, it may help them not get into so much of that credit card debt … because they will have already said ‘I need to look at these decisions. I need to make some wise choices.’”

Under the new standards, each grade level has financial literacy expectations grouped into three categories: financial awareness, risk management and money management.

In early elementary school, these standards include being able to explain the difference between spending and saving, identifying what banks do and describing factors that influence the amount of money one can spend. In high school, standards require students to identify the components of a budget, analyze different credit types and evaluate the risks and benefits of current and emerging payment options.

Prior to the 2025-2026 school year, Eldredge said there was a requirement for students to learn financial literacy as part of a social studies standard in their senior year of high school. But the learning was “buried” within other expectations, according to Eldridge.

In former K-12 State Superintendent Kirsten Baesler’s forward of the standards, she said education in the state should be about preparing students for life and that the financial literacy standards “lay out a clear, age-appropriate roadmap for students to build essential life skills.”

“Together, we are helping young North Dakotans make sound financial choices, take ownership of their futures, and thrive in a complex world,” Baesler said. “One of the most important ways we can do that is by equipping our students with the knowledge and skills to manage their personal finances with confidence and clarity.”

ADVERTISEMENT

At the local level, Grand Forks Public Schools Chief Academic Officer Amy Bartsch said school leaders felt “very comfortable” introducing the new standards into classrooms as financial literacy classes were already available under the state’s Choice Ready framework, which requires all North Dakota students after graduation to be prepared to choose from three options: higher education, workforce and military.

Grand Forks Viking Elementary Principal Loren Hoheisel emphasizes the importance of introducing financial literacy in school, and not just relying on family systems or outside environments.

“Not every family is equipped with organizational skills or financial skills or literacy skills, so we can't assume that everyone knows,” Hoheisel said.

Eldredge said no matter a student's age, they are dealing with money and questions about the best way to use their money, so starting literacy young can help them build healthy mindsets.

“If you look at our young students, we have little children who are getting gifts of money, and they make financial decisions,” she said. “They are going to have to understand how their money works and what kinds of decisions they can make with their money, and the impact that it will have on them later on."

The standards were written and reviewed by separate committees, which Eldredge said were made up of teachers who have applied or been nominated to give their perspectives on the curriculum. The process began in October 2024, the result of a partnership between NDDPI, the Bank of North Dakota and the Department of Career and Technical Education.

local_articles.title: In digital world, we need material literacy, too
local_articles.summary: In digital world, we need material literacy, too  The Michigan Daily
local_articles.full_content:
[Skip to content](https://www.michigandaily.com/opinion/columns/in-digital-world-we-need-material-literacy-too/#main)

[Home](https://www.michigandaily.com/) » [Opinion](https://www.michigandaily.com/opinion/) » [Columns](https://www.michigandaily.com/opinion/columns/) » In digital world, we need material literacy, too

![A scarf with an illegible clothing tag surrounded by question marks.](https://i0.wp.com/www.michigandaily.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/A-digital-world-we-need-material-literacy-too.png?fit=780%2C520&ssl=1)Ella Flores/DAILY

[“Media literacy”](https://www.alda-europe.eu/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/ALDA-Media-Literacy.pdf) is a popular term these days, especially online. The end of last year saw the phrase reach its zenith in [online searches](https://trends.google.com/explore?q=media%2520literacy&date=2021-01-15%202026-01-15&geo=US) following a steady, five-year increase. This growing attention is not unfounded; in an age of [decreasing](https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2025/09/whats-driving-decline-in-u-s-literacy-rates/) literacy and a heavy [reliance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563224002206) on artificial intelligence, the way we understand all the media surrounding us demands careful attention and thoughtfulness.

Parallel to media literacy is [material literacy](https://press.princeton.edu/ideas/the-need-for-material-literacy), which is in a crisis of its own. Material literacy means understanding the life cycles of our material goods, such as our clothing. The decline in [home economics classes](https://www.npr.org/sections/thesalt/2018/06/14/618329461/despite-a-revamped-focus-on-real-life-skills-home-ec-classes-fade-away) and [home garment sewing](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/casp.70172) signifies a growing distance between people and the things they own, and the normalization of fast fashion only serves to quicken that divide. As shoppers buy large quantities of low-cost clothing from sites like [Shein](https://www.elle.com.au/fashion/fashion-news/why-is-shein-so-bad-27846/), [Temu](https://goodonyou.eco/how-ethical-is-temu/) or [Forever 21](https://goodonyou.eco/how-ethical-is-forever-21/), they also overlook what makes up a garment, how it is made and who’s making it.

The shirt you’re wearing right now could be cotton or polyester or some sort of blend, and being materially literate means discerning each fabric in terms of comfort or longevity. Similarly, if an article of clothing were to tear or lose a button, having practical knowledge of your material goods means knowing what you’ll need for repairs and how to mend a garment yourself.

While interest in material literacy remains low when [compared](https://trends.google.com/explore?q=media%2520literacy%2Cmaterial%2520literacy&date=2021-01-15%202026-01-15&geo=US) with media literacy, both concerns indicate a widespread abstraction from sources of everyday necessities. Be it intangible information or material clothing, these components of daily life are available to us near-instantly because of the nature of our digital world.

In the present day, fast fashion brands make large quantities of clothing available with a click. Making these purchases is appealing to consumers because it’s cheap, simple and easy, requiring little engagement with the process spanning from creation to purchase and obscuring the dire issues posed to both [workers](https://www.business-humanrights.org/en/from-us/press-releases/fast-fashion-and-luxury-brands-shift-orders-in-hunt-for-cheaper-labour-impacting-thousands-of-garment-workers/) and the [environment](https://news.un.org/en/story/2025/03/1161636).

Just as it’s important to competently analyze and evaluate the ideas we receive through media, it’s crucial that we’re also able to empower ourselves to make, repair and purchase ethically made goods produced with quality and durability in mind.

Our current context of clothing poses a sharp contrast to the relationship people had with their garments for most of human history. Up until very recently, [craftsmanship](https://asq.org/quality-resources/history-of-quality) — not shipping — was how people obtained their coats or kirtles or shoes. Most things you wore were made yourself, or, at least, by people whom you knew.

Circumstances began to shift with the [Industrial Revolution](https://americanhistory.si.edu/explore/exhibitions/american-enterprise/online/corporate-era/mass-production), which allowed for mass quantities of goods to be produced efficiently and made costs more affordable to consumers. Still, even into the 20th century, consumers [made](https://lithub.com/a-brief-history-of-mass-manufactured-clothing/) most of their clothing at home instead of purchasing ensembles that were mass-produced and ready to wear. The 1920s began a shift toward a reliance on off-the-rack clothing, and by the 1970s, that production was predominantly in countries in the Global South. This change served to create further distance between maker, goods and consumer, leading us to the current material illiteracy we’re experiencing now.

If we’re to acknowledge the increasing importance of analyzing the media surrounding us, then we ought to scrutinize the clothes we wear everyday, too. Engaging with our personal, material belongings means engaging with the material world at large. It means empowering ourselves to connect with, make, purchase and wear long-lasting and high-quality clothing that serves us for a lifetime. Becoming materially literate also means demanding garments of a higher quality, rather than let ourselves waste money on things that are cheap, flimsy and unethical.

Awareness of these issues, though, doesn’t mean incorporating material literacy into our lives will be simple. The option of fast fashion is supposed to make things simpler for the consumer in terms of time, money and effort. Purchasing well-made, ethically produced clothing involves not only a greater expense, but careful research into brand morals and sourcing.

Additionally, repairing or even making our own clothing requires time and money, as well as expertise that demands practice. Most crafty hobbies, unfortunately, come at no small [cost](https://www.artshub.co.uk/news/features/how-much-is-your-craft-hobby-costing-and-three-ways-to-save-2614810/). But, when we invest into well-made clothing for ourselves, we’re not only making a decision more friendly to garment workers or the environment; we’re also investing in the longevity of our wardrobes. Shopping with material literacy in mind might require a higher cost upfront, but the durability of better-made garments means fewer small — but frequent — purchases in the long-term.

Material literacy doesn’t necessitate purchasing the nicest sewing machine or a collection of patterns to remake a perfect wardrobe. Material literacy instead begins with something small. Start by replacing a lost button or patching a pair of jeans or darning a sock. Then, learn to readjust a hem, or try out the Japanese mending art of [Sashiko](https://japanobjects.com/features/sashiko).

The next time you shop for clothes, take the time to read the care tag and assess the stitching or symmetry. Maybe, after a while, you’ll even feel inspired to try knitting, crocheting or sewing something of your own. Media literacy, and even basic literacy, aren’t skills you acquire overnight. Taking into account the quality of your personal wardrobe and appreciating the time that goes into crafting and wear is a good place to start.

_Audra Woehle is an Opinion Columnist and can be reached at_ [_awoehle@umich.edu_](mailto:awoehle@umich.edu) _. In her column “In Hindsight,” she writes about what our relationship to history says about us today._

## LRHS reflects on successful Literacy Week, Florida

Lakewood Ranch High School

February 5, 2026

**Carys Teich** \- Mustangs Ahead

**(LAKEWOOD RANCH, FL)- Last week, Jan. 26 –30, the state of Florida celebrated literacy week, with the theme being “Red, White, and blue: celebrating 250 years chapters of America!”**

To celebrate, LRHS Media Specialist Audrey Dombroski hosted both a challenge and contest at the LRHS library.

The challenge was for the school to check out 250 books in one week. The goal was surpassed and Mustangs checked out338 books.

Every student that checked out a book during the week was given a raffle ticket to put their name and number on for a chance to win a $10 amazon gift card. There ended up being 10 winners -

- Mikayla Jackson

- Laura Staddler

- Penny Morrris

- Jazari Morgan

- Aaron Cortez

- Sofia Galdamez

- Santiago Behrens

- Arianna Smith

- Mahari Pompey

- Crystal Chhun


Dombrowski also held a bookmark design contest. Students who wanted to participate could illustrate or paint a bookmark to go along with the literacy theme “Red, White, and blue: celebrating 250 years chapters of America!”

Bookmarks could be submitted by either dropping them off at the library or emailing a digital file to Dombrowski.

Assistant principals Samantha Biggs, Dr. Lindy Carlson, and Jessica Satterly judged Isabella Harell as the first-place winner for the grand prize, and Bella Duke as the second-place winner.

Harell was awarded $30 worth of Target and Amazon gift cards, and Duke was awarded $20 worth of Target gift cards.

“My hope is that these contests celebrating literacy week will increase the desire for students to keep coming back to check out more books and increase reading!” said Dombrowski.

# News Literacy Week brings films, talks, tips to Northern Nevada

[![Portrait of Jeffrey Meehan](https://www.rgj.com/gcdn/authoring/authoring-images/2024/01/10/PREN/72179188007-ren-jeffrey-meehan-01.JPG?crop=2649,2652,x0,y0&width=48&height=48&format=pjpg&auto=webp) Jeffrey Meehan](https://www.rgj.com/staff/72088978007/jeffrey-meehan/)

Reno Gazette Journal

Feb. 2, 2026, 5:00 a.m. PT

[Share to Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/dialog/share?display=popup&app_id=178959168788968&href=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.rgj.com%2Fstory%2Fnews%2F2026%2F02%2F02%2Fnews-literacy-week-reno%2F88434305007%2F)[Share to Twitter](https://x.com/intent/post?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.rgj.com%2Fstory%2Fnews%2F2026%2F02%2F02%2Fnews-literacy-week-reno%2F88434305007%2F&text=News%20Literacy%20Week%20brings%20films%2C%20talks%2C%20tips%20to%20Northern%20Nevada&via=rgj)[Share by email](mailto:?subject=News%20Literacy%20Week%20brings%20films%2C%20talks%2C%20tips%20to%20Northern%20Nevada%20-%20from%20Reno%20Gazette%20Journal&body=News%20Literacy%20Week%20brings%20films%2C%20talks%2C%20tips%20to%20Northern%20Nevada%0A%0ANews%20Literacy%20Week%20runs%20Feb.%202%E2%80%936%20with%20free%20films%2C%20panels%20and%20tips%20aimed%20at%20helping%20Northern%20Nevadans%20better%20judge%20news%20and%20misinformation.%0A%0ACheck%20out%20this%20story%20on%20rgj.com%3A%20https%3A%2F%2Fwww.rgj.com%2Fstory%2Fnews%2F2026%2F02%2F02%2Fnews-literacy-week-reno%2F88434305007%2F)

National News Literacy Week runs Feb. 2–6 in Northern Nevada, with free screenings, discussions and other public events.

The observance is part of the [seventh annual event](https://newslit.org/news-literacy-week/), presented nationally by the [News Literacy Project](https://newslit.org/) along with [The E.W. Scripps Co.](https://scripps.com/) and [USA TODAY Network](https://www.usatoday.com/), which includes the Reno Gazette Journal.

The nationwide event is focused on helping people better judge the credibility of news and information.

In Northern Nevada, events are being coordinated by the [News Literate Community Initiative](https://nlcwashoe.org/), a newly formed nonprofit focused on news literacy.

Citing what he described as a fractured media landscape, Steve Mulvenon, a member of the initiative’s steering committee, said studies have pointed toward an all-time low for trust in the media, with higher trust in local over legacy media.

"What we had observed is that average citizens are just having a hard time differentiating fact from fiction and honest news from opinion. And recently, with AI, it's become increasingly difficult to figure out what to believe," he said.

A free screening of the documentary "Trust Me," which explores media trust and misinformation, will be held Tuesday, Feb. 3, at the Mathewson-IGT Knowledge Center, at the Wells Fargo Auditorium, University of Nevada, Reno. Additional events include a wait-listed film screening and panel discussion at PBS Reno and a public webinar on how residents can become trained community ambassadors for news literacy.

Throughout the week, KUNR will air tips on identifying misinformation and evaluating sources.

## News Literacy Week events open to the public, Feb. 2-6

Here's how Northern Nevadans can get involved during News Literacy Week:

**News Literate Community Pledge:** Residents can sign the News Literate Community Pledge at [nlcwashoe.org/pledge](http://nlcwashoe.org/pledge) to show support for responsible news consumption and media awareness. The pledge is free and open throughout the week.

**Free documentary screening: "Trust Me":** University Libraries at the University of Nevada, Reno will host a free screening of "Trust Me," an award-winning documentary exploring media trust and truth, from 3:30-5:10 p.m. Tuesday, Feb. 3, at the Mathewson-IGT Knowledge Center, Wells Fargo Auditorium, 1664 N. Virginia St., Reno. No registration is required.

**Documentary screening and panel discussion:** PBS Reno will host a screening of "Fake: Searching for Truth in the Age of Misinformation," followed by a panel discussion from 6-8 p.m. Wednesday, Feb. 4. The event is currently wait-list only.

**How to become a News Literate Community Ambassador:** A free public webinar introducing the News Literate Community Initiative’s ambassador training program will be held Thursday, Feb. 5. Registration is available at [nlcwashoe.org](http://nlcwashoe.org/).

**News literacy tips on KUNR:** Throughout News Literacy Week, KUNR will air short daily tips developed by the News Literate Community Initiative, offering listeners practical ways to identify misinformation.

# Highlights from ELLOK Annual Conference 2026: Health Literacy in Focus: Opportunities and Challenges

[The Hellenic Cancer Federation – ELLOK](https://ellok.org/en/) organized its 10th Annual Conference on 2, 3 and 4 February 2026, entitled **_“People at the Center – Building Bridges of Care”_**, at the Wyndham Grand Athens hotel, with the participation of leading representatives of the State, the scientific and academic community, the health industry and patient organisations from Greece and abroad.

The Conference was held in conjunction with World Cancer Day (4 February) and served as a reference point for highlighting key public health and cancer care issues, at a time marked on the one hand by international instability and growing pressure on health systems, and on the other by significant progress made in Greece in cancer prevention, diagnosis and treatment over recent years.

Within this framework, the session **_“Health Literacy in Focus: Opportunities and Challenges”_** brought together **Antonis Billis, Christos Tsagkaris, Nikolina Dodlek,** and **Marius Geanta,** moderated by **Andreas Charalambous**, to explore how health literacy can reduce inequalities, strengthen prevention and early detection, and support more informed and person-centered decision-making across the cancer continuum.

## Health Literacy as a Driver of Equity in Cancer Care

A central theme throughout the discussion was that health literacy is not limited to individual knowledge or education. It functions as a structural determinant of access and equity, influencing whether people engage in prevention strategies, participate in screening and early detection, and feel able to understand treatment options and follow care pathways.

The session emphasized that limited health literacy can deepen existing disparities, particularly among communities facing socioeconomic barriers, cultural and language obstacles, or reduced access to reliable information channels. In this sense, improving health literacy was presented not only as a communication goal, but as a meaningful route toward more equitable cancer care.

## Designing Solutions That Last

Nikolina Dodlek highlighted the importance of building health literacy initiatives that are designed for implementation and sustainability rather than remaining isolated, short-term interventions. She described a structured approach aimed at developing training and educational materials that can be maintained over time, adapted to different user groups, and strengthened through continuous input.

A key point in her contribution was that effective health literacy work should be user-centered, meaning it must actively involve the people it intends to serve. The session stressed the value of real-time feedback and engagement, so that educational resources respond to genuine needs, reflect lived experiences, and remain practical in everyday healthcare settings.

![ELLOK - OncoDaily](https://oncodaily.com/pub/uploads/2026/02/2485-533x800.webp)

## A System-Level Approach to Cancer Literacy and Inequalities

Marius Geanta expanded the conversation by framing health literacy as a system capability, not solely an individual skill. He presented a European-focused approach targeting inequalities in cancer care that are shaped by gaps in understanding and communication across populations.

In his remarks, health literacy was positioned as a bridge between citizens, patients, healthcare professionals, and institutions, with the aim of making prevention, early detection, and treatment pathways more accessible and navigable. Rather than treating cancer literacy as a single intervention, he described it as a coordinated effort that must occur across multiple levels of the healthcare ecosystem.

![ELLOK - OncoDaily](https://oncodaily.com/pub/uploads/2026/02/2489-800x533.webp)

## From Citizens to Institutions

The session underlined that improving cancer literacy begins at the population level, where clear and actionable information can support prevention, awareness, and participation in screening. It then becomes even more critical within clinical care, where patients must understand options, benefits and risks, and the reasoning behind recommendations in order to engage in shared decision-making.

Speakers also drew attention to the responsibility of healthcare professionals to communicate with clarity and empathy, noting that health literacy is shaped by the way information is delivered, not only by the capacity of patients to interpret it. Equally important was the role of organizations and systems, which should be designed in ways that reduce confusion and administrative barriers, creating environments that are easier to navigate, especially for those at higher risk of being left behind.

## Digital Transformation and The Emerging Role of AI

A forward-looking element of the discussion addressed the growing influence of digital tools on health literacy. The session acknowledged that digital health resources can expand reach and provide scalable education, but also warned that digital transformation can worsen inequalities if access to technology and digital skills are uneven.

Within this context, the conversation referenced the potential role of AI-driven tools, including large language models, as emerging supports for information access and understanding. At the same time, speakers emphasized that these tools must be approached carefully, with attention to reliability, safety, and fairness, so that innovation reduces rather than reinforces disparities.

![ELLOK - OncoDaily](https://oncodaily.com/pub/uploads/2026/02/2499-533x800.webp)

## Opportunities and Challenges in Moving from Discussion to Action

Across the contributions, the session offered a balanced view of the field. The opportunities were presented as real and actionable, especially when education is structured, training is sustained, and development is guided by collaboration with users. At the same time, the challenges were described as persistent and multi-layered, including unequal access to understandable information, complexity of healthcare systems, communication gaps, information overload, and digital divides that affect vulnerable populations. The overall message was that progress requires coordinated solutions that operate simultaneously at the individual, professional, organizational, and policy levels.

[![WTOC meteorologist Jamie Ertle poses for a photo with a class at Richmond Hill Elementary School after reading Never Teach A Stegosaurus To Do Sums](https://www.savannahtribune.com/wp-content/uploads/images/2026-02-04/7p1-1024x534.jpg)](https://www.savannahtribune.com/wp-content/uploads/images/2026-02-04/7p1.jpg) WTOC meteorologist Jamie Ertle poses for a photo with a class at Richmond Hill Elementary School after reading Never Teach A Stegosaurus To Do Sums

United Way of the Coastal Empire hosted Read United Day across the five-county region — including Bryan, Chatham, Effingham, Liberty and Long counties — on Jan. 30. More than 1,000 volunteers read aloud to more than 20,000 students in pre-K through second grade and provided each classroom with a new book to add to its library.

Read United Day is part of Read United, a year-round series of programs designed to improve early language and literacy for all children in United Way’s service area.

“Read United Day is about more than a single moment in the classroom. It is about setting children up for long-term success,” said Cheri Dean, vice president of direct services and impact at United Way of the Coastal Empire. “The National School Boards Association reminds us that by the end of third grade, children should shift from learning to read to reading to learn. When we invest in literacy early, we are helping build stronger outcomes for students and stronger communities for all of us.”

“When children are supported early with strong literacy experiences, they are better prepared to succeed in school and beyond,” said Kimberly Waller, president and CEO of United Way of the Coastal Empire. “Thanks to our volunteers and partners, we are helping build a stronger future for our children and our community, one story at a time.”

This year’s Read United Day was made possible through the generous support of sponsors, led by premier partner WTOC and platinum sponsors Gateway Terminals, Liberty County School System and Nestlé Health Science. Gold sponsors include Colonial Group, Georgia Ports Authority, Gulfstream and Publix, with additional support from silver sponsors EOM, Georgia Power and Rotary Club of Savannah. Bronze sponsors include GAF, Great Dane, Port City Logistics and VyStar Credit Union. Friends sponsors include Rotary Club of Metropolitan Savannah and Rotary Club of Savannah East, and Live Oak Fiber is a Readers sponsor. Special thanks to Dollar General Literacy Foundation for its grant support.

# Join Literacy Pittsburgh: Volunteer as an English tutor

By Rachel Windsor on February 2, 2026

When Crafton resident Rachel Stalker wanted to make a consistent contribution to her community, she knew just the place.

Armed with some experience in the realm of English as a second language instruction, Stalker started volunteering with Literacy Pittsburgh in 2025 as an English language learning tutor.

“I was looking for some community service or volunteer opportunity that felt regular,” Stalker said. “Something I could invest time into.”

Stalker tutors out of the West End Library and is matched with students through Literacy Pittsburgh. The nonprofit provides free adult and youth education and services across Allegheny County, including English language learning and adult education for individuals seeking to improve reading and writing skills, or prepare for a high school equivalency credential exam.

Literacy Pittsburgh also serves children, but there’s a much higher need for adult program volunteers, said Maria Polinsky, director of marketing and communications for the nonprofit.

“The biggest need is individuals who are willing to meet one-on-one or in small groups with students out in their community,” Polinsky explained.

According to Literacy Pittsburgh, demand for services in the western city neighborhoods and suburbs continues to outpace the number of available tutors.

![](https://i0.wp.com/westhillsgazette.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/Literacy-Pittsburgh-1-020126-jpg.webp?resize=480%2C640&quality=80&ssl=1)Volunteer tutor Barb Hopkins teaches English Language Learning students. (Photo courtesy of Literacy Pittsburgh)

At the moment, more than 200 people in the county – including 24 in the West Hills – are on a waitlist for English language tutoring through Literacy Pittsburgh.

Over the past three years, Literacy Pittsburgh West programs have served 613 students, the majority of whom are English language learners between the ages of 25 and 64. More than half of students are parents, and many balance employment and family responsibilities with tutoring.

During Literacy Pittsburgh West’s 2024-25 program year, 45% of participating students kept or obtained employment, 57% advanced their education level and two students earned a high school equivalency credential.

To become a tutor, Stalker attended an informational session and completed the organization’s required training. She previously worked as a teaching assistant for English as a second language classes while in college, an experience that drew her to this role.

After completing training and meeting with a program coordinator, Stalker was matched with students in her area. She currently works with two adult learners, meeting about twice a week for a total of three to four hours.

Literacy Pittsburgh provides tutors and students with curriculum materials and ongoing support.

While tutors are required to be at least 18 years old and have a high school diploma or equivalent, prior teaching experience is not required.

![](https://i0.wp.com/westhillsgazette.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/Literacy-Pittsburgh-2-020126-jpg.webp?resize=1024%2C683&quality=80&ssl=1)New volunteers learn about teaching adult learners at a Tutor Training session. (Photo courtesy of Literacy Pittsburgh)

“We always say that patience and enthusiasm are much more important than teaching experience,” Polinsky said. “Their program coordinator is always available for questions and to help them move forward if they get stuck.”

Stalker said the most meaningful part of her experience has been working with people in her own community.

“Meeting somebody who’s part of my community, that maybe is outside of my circle and getting to invest in their growth, that’s been really why I wanted to do something like this,” she said.

Polinsky said one of the things that she and others affiliated with the program hear most from volunteers is that this is a life-changing experience for them as well as their students.

“They’re learning about different cultures,” Polinsky said of the tutors. “They’re learning about different ways of life.

“We once had a tutor say, ‘I know I was told that I would open up a life, but I didn’t expect it to be my life.’”

For those interested in volunteering, Literacy Pittsburgh will hold a new volunteer orientation on Saturday, Feb. 14, at the West End Library. Additional information about volunteer requirements and upcoming trainings is available at [literacypittsburgh.org](https://literacypittsburgh.org/ "").
